title: What Shareholder Rights Do You Get?
objectives: List the main rights of an ordinary shareholder
Explain how voting rights work at AGMs
Describe what a company annual report tells investors

vocabulary.definition: The standard type of share. Holders get voting rights, dividends (if declared), and a share of assets if the company winds up — but only after debts are paid.
A yearly meeting where shareholders vote on company matters — electing directors, approving auditors, voting on major decisions.
A detailed document published by listed companies each year showing financial results, strategy, risks and director remuneration.
The official list of all shareholders in a company, maintained by a registrar. Shows who owns how many shares.
A payment from a company to shareholders that returns capital rather than profits. Different from a dividend — a capital return reduces the company's share capital.

activities.title: What Do You Actually Own?
activities.notePrompt: Write: If you own 1,000 shares out of 200,000,000 shares in a NZ company, what percentage of the company do you own? What does that give you?
activities.content:
Owning a share means owning a fraction of a company. That fraction comes with specific legal rights.

The four main rights of an ordinary shareholder:

1. Voting rights: You can vote on significant company decisions at the Annual General Meeting (AGM). One share = one vote. You can elect and remove directors, approve or reject major transactions, and vote on executive pay. If you own 1,000 shares and the company has 200 million shares, your vote is 0.0005% of the total — tiny, but it still counts.

2. Dividends: If the company's board declares a dividend, you receive cash proportional to your shareholding. The board is not obligated to pay dividends — it can decide to retain profits instead.

3. Information rights: Listed companies must publish annual reports, half-year financial results, and NZX announcements about material events. As a shareholder you have a legal right to receive this information.

4. Residual claim: If the company is wound up, you receive a share of remaining assets after all debts and preference shareholders are paid. In practice, liquidated company shareholders rarely receive anything — creditors take priority.

activities.title: Ordinary vs Preference Shares
activities.notePrompt: Write: What is the main trade-off between ordinary and preference shares? Which would a risk-averse income investor prefer?
activities.content:
Most NZ investors deal only in ordinary shares. But companies can also issue preference shares.

Ordinary shares: Get voting rights and dividends when declared. Dividends can vary or be skipped. If the company does well, ordinary shareholders benefit most — no cap on upside. If it fails, ordinary shareholders are last in line for any residual assets.

Preference shares: Usually have no voting rights. In exchange they get a fixed dividend that must be paid before ordinary shareholders receive anything. If the company is wound up, preference shareholders are paid out before ordinary shareholders. Think of them as somewhere between a bond (fixed income, priority) and an ordinary share (still equity, not guaranteed).

For income-seeking investors, preference shares offer more predictability. For growth investors, ordinary shares offer unlimited upside. Most NZX-listed companies only have ordinary shares — preference shares are more common in private companies and some large Australian banks.

activities.statement: Ordinary shareholders are the first to be paid out when a company goes bankrupt and its assets are sold.
activities.explanation: False. Ordinary shareholders are last in the priority queue during liquidation. The correct order is: (1) secured creditors (banks with security over assets), (2) unsecured creditors (suppliers, bondholders), (3) preference shareholders, (4) ordinary shareholders. In practice, most company liquidations return nothing to ordinary shareholders — by the time everyone else is paid, the assets are exhausted.

activities.title: The AGM — Where Shareholders Exercise Power
activities.notePrompt: Write: What decisions are typically made at an AGM? Could a small shareholder with 0.01% of votes actually influence anything?
activities.content:
Every listed NZ company must hold an Annual General Meeting (AGM) each year. It is the main formal mechanism through which shareholders exercise oversight over company management.

Typical AGM agenda items:
— Re-election of directors
— Approval of the auditor and their fees
— Approval of director remuneration
— Voting on any proposed capital restructuring or major asset sale
— Shareholder questions to management

Proxy voting: If you cannot attend in person, you can submit a proxy vote online. Most NZ share registries (Computershare, Link Market Services) allow online voting through their portals. Many small shareholders never vote — which is why large institutional investors (KiwiSaver funds, pension funds) have enormous effective influence.

Activist shareholders: Occasionally a shareholder with significant holdings (5%+) will campaign for change at an AGM — voting against director re-election, proposing strategic changes, or pushing for higher dividends. This is called shareholder activism and has led to real governance changes at NZ companies.

questions.question: A company has 500,000,000 ordinary shares. You own 250,000. What is your voting power at the AGM?
options: 0.05% — a small but real voting stake proportional to your shareholding
25% — because you own shares in a $250,000 investment
0% — small shareholders have no voting rights
questions.explanation: 250,000 ÷ 500,000,000 × 100 = 0.05%. You have a genuine vote, but 0.05% of it. For most company decisions at an AGM, your individual vote is unlikely to be decisive. Your power as a small shareholder lies primarily in collective action — if many small shareholders vote together — or through the indirect pressure of institutional investors (like KiwiSaver funds) who vote much larger blocks of shares.

questions.question: A NZ company goes into receivership with $50 million in assets. Secured creditors are owed $40 million. Preference shareholders are owed $12 million. What do ordinary shareholders receive?
options: $10 million split proportionally among ordinary shareholders
Nothing — secured creditors take $40M leaving $10M, insufficient for the $12M preference shareholders owed, so ordinary shareholders receive nothing
$2 million — the remainder after all priority creditors are paid
The same amount per share as preference shareholders
questions.explanation: $50M assets minus $40M secured creditors = $10M remaining. Preference shareholders are owed $12M but only $10M is left, so they receive approximately 83 cents per dollar owed — and ordinary shareholders receive nothing at all. This illustrates why investing in companies with large debt loads is risky for ordinary shareholders: even modest asset shortfalls wipe them out completely.

activities.title: Ordinary or Preference Share? Sort These Features

pairs.item: Fixed dividend that must be paid before ordinary shareholders
Unlimited upside if company grows rapidly
Priority over ordinary shares in a liquidation
Dividend can be skipped by the board if profits are insufficient
No voting rights in exchange for greater income security

activities.explanation: Preference shares trade upside potential and voting control for income predictability and priority in bad scenarios. Ordinary shares offer full participation in company growth and voting rights at the cost of being last in line if things go wrong. Most retail investors on the NZX deal exclusively in ordinary shares.

activities.statement: NZ listed companies are required to pay dividends to shareholders every six months.
activities.explanation: False. No company is legally required to pay dividends. The board of directors decides whether to pay a dividend, how much, and when, based on the company's financial position and capital requirements. Many NZ growth companies pay no dividend at all, preferring to reinvest all profits. Even companies that have paid regular dividends can suspend them if conditions change — Air New Zealand suspended its dividend during COVID-19 as an example.

activities.question: What is the official annual meeting called where shareholders vote on company matters including director elections?
activities.placeholder: Type the full name or abbreviation...
activities.hint: Three letters — or write the full three-word name.
activities.explanation: The Annual General Meeting (AGM) is the main mechanism through which shareholders exercise oversight over company management. NZ listed companies are legally required to hold an AGM each year. Most NZ shareholders can now vote online through share registrar portals without attending in person.

activities.title: Would You Vote?
activities.prompt: If you owned shares in a NZ company and the AGM was voting on whether to pay the CEO $4.5 million per year, would you vote? What information would you want before deciding? Write three sentences about what factors you would consider.
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — understanding shareholder rights is part of being an informed investor.
